executiveSummary:
# Executive Summary: Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

National Medicaid enrollment has stabilized at approximately 75.3 million beneficiaries as of January 2026, with most major states experiencing modest enrollment declines following the end of pandemic-era continuous coverage protections. California and Illinois saw the steepest year-over-year decreases at 6.2% each, while Michigan experienced a concerning 7.6% decline. Texas, with 4.0 million enrollees, recorded a relatively moderate 3.1% decrease, mirroring Pennsylvania's performance and outperforming neighboring states like Ohio (-4.4%). North Carolina stands as the notable exception with slight growth of 0.5%, suggesting successful retention strategies worth examining. National spending reached $216 billion in fiscal year 2024, with a per-enrollee average of $2,869, though significant variation exists across states ranging from Florida's efficient $2,031 per enrollee to Pennsylvania's high-cost $4,377 per enrollee.

Texas demonstrates mixed performance relative to peer states, spending $2,988 per enrollee—slightly above the national average but well below high-cost states like Pennsylvania and Ohio. The state's managed care penetration of 93.3% exceeds the national average of 83.2%, indicating strong care coordination infrastructure. However, Texas quality performance remains a concern, scoring 62 out of 100 and ranking 30th nationally, placing it in the bottom half despite serving the nation's third-largest Medicaid population. This moderate quality performance lags behind leading states like Massachusetts (81) and Minnesota (80), and suggests opportunities for improvement in care delivery and outcomes measurement. With active federal oversight continuing and new HCBS quality measures being implemented for 2028, states should prioritize quality improvement initiatives while maintaining cost efficiency and enrollment stability.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

Texas operates the third-largest Medicaid program nationally with 4.03 million enrollees, representing a significant portion of the state's healthcare safety net. The program's enrollment declined 3.1% year-over-year, aligning closely with the national trend as states complete post-pandemic eligibility redeterminations. At $2,988 per enrollee, Texas spends 4.2% above the national average of $2,868, positioning it in the middle tier among large states—higher than Florida ($2,031) and North Carolina ($2,616) but well below expansion states like New York ($3,431) and Pennsylvania ($4,377). This spending pattern reflects Texas's continued non-expansion status, which limits coverage to the most acute cases and typically drives higher per-capita costs for the enrolled population. The state's managed care penetration of 93.3% significantly exceeds the national average of 83.2%, demonstrating strong commitment to coordinated care delivery models.

However, quality outcomes present a concerning picture that demands immediate attention. Texas ranks 30th nationally with a quality score of 62/100, placing it in the bottom tier among large states and below the national average of 65.6. While this keeps Texas out of the lowest-performing category occupied by several other Southern non-expansion states like Arkansas (54) and South Carolina (53), it significantly lags behind peer states like North Carolina (rank and score not provided but likely higher given their lower spending and positive enrollment growth). The combination of above-average spending with below-average quality outcomes suggests potential inefficiencies in care delivery that warrant investigation. Areas to monitor include whether the high managed care penetration translates into improved quality metrics over time, how Texas compares to other non-expansion states on specific quality measures, and whether targeted quality improvement initiatives can close the gap with higher-performing states while maintaining cost discipline.

alerts.title: Pennsylvania Per-Enrollee Spending 53% Above National Average
alerts.description: Pennsylvania's per-enrollee spending of $4,377 is significantly higher than the national average of $2,869, indicating potential cost efficiency concerns.

alerts.title: Florida Extremely Low Per-Enrollee Spending Raises Questions
alerts.description: Florida's per-enrollee spending of $2,031 is 29% below the national average, potentially indicating inadequate service provision or unusual cost structures.

alerts.title: Five States Show Critically Low Quality Scores
alerts.description: Arkansas, South Carolina, Alabama, Oklahoma, and Mississippi all scored below 55 on quality measures, well below the national average of 65.6.

alerts.title: Texas High Spending with Moderate Quality Performance
alerts.description: Texas spends above the national average ($2,988 vs $2,869 per enrollee) but achieves only moderate quality scores (62/100, rank #30).

executiveInsights.title: TX quality score at 62/100 ranks #30 nationally - critical improvement needed
executiveInsights.summary: Texas scored 62/100 on quality measures, ranking 30th out of 50 states in the moderate tier. This is well below the national average of 65.6 and far from high-performer threshold of 75+.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Quality performance directly impacts contract renewals, federal oversight, and potential penalties. This positions Texas as a prime candidate for quality improvement initiatives.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag quality improvement opportunities to HHSC leadership immediately

executiveInsights.title: TX per-enrollee costs at $2,988 vs national average $2,869 - cost management opportunity
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $2,988 per enrollee compared to the national average of $2,869, representing $119 above average per member. With 4M+ enrollees, this represents significant excess spending potential.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Higher per-enrollee costs signal potential inefficiencies that could be addressed through better care management, especially given Texas's high managed care penetration at 93.3%.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review cost management strategies with HHSC finance team

executiveInsights.title: TX enrollment declined 3.1% YoY while NC grew 0.5% - redetermination impact
executiveInsights.summary: Texas lost 3.1% of enrollees year-over-year while North Carolina actually grew by 0.5%. Texas decline aligns with national trend but NC's growth suggests different approach to redeterminations.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Enrollment changes affect revenue projections and system capacity planning. Understanding why NC bucked the trend could inform Texas retention strategies.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review NC's redetermination processes for potential Texas application

executiveInsights.title: New HCBS quality measures for 2028 published in Federal Register
executiveInsights.summary: CMS published new Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services Quality Measure Set for 2028, indicating upcoming regulatory changes. This affects quality reporting requirements and potentially reimbursement.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: New quality measures will impact Texas's already moderate quality performance and may require system updates, staff training, and process changes well before 2028 implementation.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag new HCBS quality requirements to HHSC compliance team

executiveInsights.title: TX managed care penetration at 93.3% exceeds national 83.2% average
executiveInsights.summary: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration with 3.86M enrollees in managed care, significantly above the national average of 83.2%. This represents strong managed care adoption but may limit further efficiency gains.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: High managed care penetration suggests limited room for traditional managed care expansion strategies, requiring focus on optimizing existing managed care performance instead.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review managed care optimization opportunities rather than expansion strategies

riskOpportunity.label: EVV system renewal
riskOpportunity.description: Texas HHSC's Electronic Visit Verification system contract approaching renewal with potential for expansion or competitive rebid.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Proactively engage HHSC procurement team to position for upcoming EVV opportunities.

riskOpportunity.label: Quality measure gap
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks 30th nationally with 62/100 quality score, creating pressure for system improvements and consulting needs.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop quality improvement roadmap targeting specific Texas performance gaps.

riskOpportunity.label: Enrollment decline impact
riskOpportunity.description: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped 3.1% year-over-year, potentially reducing program scope and budget allocations.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Analyze enrollment trends to identify efficiency opportunities and cost optimization strategies.

riskOpportunity.label: HCBS quality measures
riskOpportunity.description: New 2028 Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services quality measures will require system updates and compliance efforts.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Position as HCBS quality measurement implementation partner for Texas HHSC.

riskOpportunity.label: OIG oversight risk
riskOpportunity.description: Active HHS OIG Medicaid oversight activities create compliance risks but also opportunities for audit remediation work.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Strengthen compliance monitoring capabilities and develop OIG response protocols.

riskOpportunity.label: Managed care optimization
riskOpportunity.description: Texas's 93.3% managed care penetration exceeds national average, creating opportunities for MCO support and optimization.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop managed care analytics and optimization solutions for Texas MCO ecosystem.

riskOpportunity.label: Competitive procurement pressure
riskOpportunity.description: Increased procurement transparency through ESBD and multiple bidding platforms intensifies competition for Texas contracts.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Enhance competitive intelligence and proposal win rate through improved market monitoring.

riskOpportunity.label: Data collection expansion
riskOpportunity.description: New CMS generic information collection activities for Medicaid and CHIP will require enhanced data management capabilities.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Build data collection and reporting capabilities to support expanded CMS requirements.

pulseMetrics.label: Total Enrollment
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas has 4,032,864 Medicaid enrollees as of January 2026.

pulseMetrics.label: Per Enrollee Spend
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas spends $2,988 per Medicaid enrollee annually in fiscal year 2024.

pulseMetrics.label: Managed Care Rate
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration with 3,863,808 managed care enrollees.

pulseMetrics.label: Quality Score
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas ranks 30th nationally with a moderate quality score of 62 out of 100.

pulseMetrics.label: Active Procurement Items
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Two active procurement portals track Texas HHSC and ESBD opportunities.

pulseMetrics.label: OIG/Regulatory Signals
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Five active regulatory and oversight items from HHS OIG and Federal Register.

pulseMetrics.label: Next Milestone
pulseMetrics.tooltip: No specific upcoming milestones identified in available data.
